All right, let's go into the first step. We call this market research. This is the essential first step for entrepreneurs and copywriters. When deciding how or even if you will approach a specific market or niche audience. This is very, very important. It's a step that a lot of people want to skip. They think, no, I understand this. I only need this for a new market I'm going to go into. This is not true. This is the kind of stuff that I did as a freelance copywriter. The advantage of being a freelancer like I was is that every time I took on a new job, I had to learn everything about the market, about my client, about the type of prospects that they had. I had to learn everything from scratch and I had to learn it very, very quickly. That often because I knew how to do this in a very shortcut way, I became more of an expert on the market than the client I was working for. The reason I was able to do this is because I took the simple steps of just checking into the market to see as you will see how big it is, how competitive it is, what the parameters are of it. Whether you're in a market that you've been in all your life or whether you're approaching a freelance career where you're going to have a lot of different markets or whatever it is, before you sit down to actually write anything, you've got to get a good grounding of the territory around you, where you're at, where you're going and why you're going there. In market research, the first thing we're going to talk about will be competition. The second thing we're going to talk about will be the size of the game and I'll explain that. The last thing is, is there a market? Very quickly, for competition, you need to identify who the competition is. Get on their lists online. Actually order from them. Pay attention to the process that you've been taken through when you actually place an order or when you get involved, when you call them up. Sample their goods and services, especially their customer service. Call their offices, speak to salesmen, ask questions, Google them, stock them online. This is an opportunity for you to put on your detective hat and to dig for clues, answers and insight. This is really fun when you start to get into it. There's no law against you contacting a competition. You don't need to identify yourself. You don't need to say, hey, I'm going to be looking to take away all of your market share here. You can just be a regular customer or you can call and just say, I'm interested in how you guys do business. How do you guys process everything? Start finding this stuff out. Very, very easy to do. Okay, now the size of the game. You basically have to find out how many competitors there are going after how many prospects. So part of finding this out is to find out, is there a market at all? How many prospects are there? Sometimes markets are just too small to break into efficiently. Other times a good idea just doesn't have a large enough responsive audience or it's been co-opted already and given away free. A lot of people that were selling books and information last year are now finding that their competition is giving away that information for free. So what was worth something last year is now worth essentially nothing because it's now become free in the market. You have to be able to understand how this works. One of the tactics that you can use is AdWords, for example. Look at keyword searches. If you've never used AdWords, it's very, very easy to get involved and they have excellent tools to help you decide how many people are out there looking for particular keywords, which essentially is how large the audience is that is interested in the market you're talking about. Also publication circulation. If you want to go out there and say the golf market, you can look at how big are the major golf magazines. That's an indication of how many people are interested enough to go out and buy a magazine. If you can find out how many people are checking into certain online sites, that information is a little harder to get. But any resource that you can find to gauge the audience size is going to be important. The key here is to never make an emotional decision based on passion. Work from facts. Don't say, I've got the best product for XYZ and I've dealt with people who thought that they were going to make a million dollars with products that had no perceivable value to the audience they were going to go after. It was something they were interested in and they really hoped there was going to be a market. But when we looked at the facts, there just wasn't a market out there. People weren't willing to pay the money that was needed for the person to make a profit on that business. So sometimes you just got to walk away from certain markets. So basically, it's very simple. It's a simple exercise that will take you through. Again, this is an essential part of figuring out the landscape that you're going to be working in. And we're going to get deeper into that landscape in the next couple of sessions. Hey, we're back again for the Q&A session of the market research module, the simple writing system. Just a quick reminder, as I told you in the introduction, what we've done is the thousands of people and the tens of thousands of questions that we've gotten over the years. It's just some of the most common questions, some of the what I think are the best questions. I'm going to sit in for all the thousands of people we can't bring here while we're making this product. And I'm just going to throw some questions up to John and then perhaps add some insights that I've learned from kind of coaching people through the process. Ready, John? Yeah, sure. Okay. You touched on this in the lecture, but it's, and you've done that before and the question comes in, is why should people be motivated to do this? What are they trying to accomplish or what are they trying to save themselves by going through this before they start writing to spend some time on researching their market? I was stunned as a freelancer over the years as to how many clients that I walked into their offices do a project with who had lost touch with their market. They'd been in the same market for years and years and years and they thought they had it down, but markets change, things change. This happened dramatically once the web started. Then companies were moving online and the email became a major way of marketing things. And a lot of people just got stuck in the 90s and they just didn't make that shift. And a lot of them made it slowly and then when mobile marketing started happening, then they didn't make that shift. So one of the first things I learned to ask people who are clients who are having trouble like sales were suddenly down or everything was wrong, everything that worked last year isn't working anymore, I'd say what's going on in your market? Are there new competitors? What is going on? So as a writer, again, whether you're writing for your own business or whether you're a freelancer writing for someone else, if you're in charge of writing, you need to get hip to the landscape that that's what's going on. Who are you writing to and what's going on? What has changed? Never take anything for granted. When you assume you make an ass out of you and me. And the biggest problem that marketers and writers make is they assume, they say, I kind of got that down. Things have always happened fast in business. They're happening faster than ever now. So just get a grounding. You don't need to spend a lot of time on this, but you need to be grounded in what's going on. Does that make sense? Yeah, I think it does. Okay, let's say there's one of the people out there who's taking the simple writing system, they have an idea for a product. They don't have the product yet, but they have an idea. They have a choice today. Do they want to spend time learning about the market or beginning the long, the complicated, the process of creating that product from scratch? Which should they do first? Easy. They've got to figure out if the market's there. They got to find out what's going on. If there's no competitors in a market, there was a time when the web had just gotten going that it was like, oh boy, this is a virgin territory. We can go in there and make a lot of money. That's really not the case anymore. If there is no competition, that's often a sign that the market really doesn't exist, that people aren't spending money. What was it they were talking about? There's difference between cat owners and dog owners. That's one of the classics. People will spend everything they have to make their dog comfortable. I'm a dog owner myself, and I tell you that she eats better than I do most days. Cat owners, on the other hand, though they love their pets and though they will try to pamper them, do not spend a lot of money on cat things. Cat bed, cat food, they're fine with that as long as the cat's happy. Trying to market some fancy product or information-based product or even a new type of thing to come to cat owners is an uphill battle, and that's been proven over the years. To dog owners, sometimes it's really easy to just walk in there and just say, we got a new way of doing this with dogs, new way of training them, new way of doing thunder blankets so they're okay during thunderstorms, all kinds of things. That kind of thing you can't know unless you've been in the market. Even if you're a business owner, you've had a store or you've been online for years and you think you understand the market, if you're going to sit down and write an ad, and if you're writing that ad because your old stuff isn't working or there's been some downturn in sales or anything, recheck the market. We should be doing this constantly. That's why it's step number one. I hang out with the best freelance copywriters in the world, what we call the A-list of copywriters. All of them know that step number one is research. Figure out what the landscape is that you're walking into. Go into it blindly. Okay. Kind of a segue, another common misstep. We see people who are starting out or very passionate. Could you talk a little bit more about how people let you are not your market, no matter how knowledgeable you are about training the dog? The other thing that happened with clients over the 30 years I was a freelance copywriter is when I would go in to talk to the owner of the business, I found out they were often deluded into thinking that they were the average person buying their stuff and they weren't. They knew much more about the product. They had spent years making the product or writing the information, the books. They hung out, they ate, lived and breathed the product and they took for granted way too much. They kept thinking, I wouldn't want that so I don't think my customers wouldn't want that or the prospects in the market would want that. They were almost never right. They had lost touch with the average prospect because they knew too much, they had been in it too long, they were sometimes jaded about it. One of the things that I have to do with clients is get them to not pull an ad that's still working because they're tired of it or their spouse is tired of it. They say, that old ad, it's got to be done. No, if it's still working, it's still working. Work off of facts. The market, if you're not paying attention, you may not know a new competitor has come in and he's eating your, cleaning your clock, eating your lunch, is the term I was looking for. If you don't know that, it's not shame on you. It's very difficult to keep up with this stuff so you should really stay on top of this stuff. As a person who's going to be writing the materials, you need to know if the big headline that you have or the great email subject line you have has been used before. If your competition has already ran it into the ground so it may be new to you and it may be great and all the people around you may think it's great, but if the market has already seen it or they're not responding to it, then yes, shame on you for not doing this simple research to find out what the starting, what the starting playing field is. How hip is the audience you're after? We're going to get into this, but especially look at the competition, especially look at the size of the game and of course finding out whether there is a market or not. Last question is you touched on a couple of them during the lecture, but let's just try to give people a couple specific places to go. Right now you're doing market research. Yeah, like the websites come and go, product names come and go, businesses come and go, but let's just try to give them somewhere like if they're really not sure where to start. AdWords is just so great, the keyword search tool. I'm not going to tell you how to get in there, but basically you look at how many people are looking for specific search terms or search words and they will give you actual, and this is all free still, I believe, they will give you a good idea of how many people are actively involved in this, which is essentially a market. Now the other thing as far as looking at publications, there's a thing called Standard Rates and Data Service or srds.com, you can go there. That costs money, but professionals like to, if being in publications or reaching the people who read publications, say in golf, golf digest and golf magazine are big, you have to understand what's going on in those magazines to understand the market, then it's worth getting involved with places like srds.com, which will tell you the circulation sizes, how big the mailing lists are, and they'll give you a demographic breakdown. So male to female, the ages, the income brackets, things like that. There are, and there are never changing, and it's getting better all the time online. There are multiple places you can go to to find out what the size of publications are, what the size of certain markets are. Things change rapidly, but one of the things you can start with is just Google, how do I find out how many people are involved in, you know, pet training, how many people are involved in teaching golf right now. You can start right there. That's, Mr. Google is one of the most wonderful things that has happened to us in a long time, so the search, using the search engine is a great place to start. And once you start getting into it again, this is a beginning step, it's also kind of fun, put on your detective app, you're actually finding out every sliver of information you find is going to help you. It's going to help you write, it's going to help you write better stuff, it's going to help you identify your best prospects and have that conversation you need to have with them. Yeah, the only thing I'll add to that is part of this is what your planned ad spend is going to be. If you're going to, there are companies that do this as a service, will provide this type of information. If you're talking about spending six, seven figures on advertising in the near future, that might be a reasonable investment. For most businesses, you don't need to make that investment up front. You really can, just by being inquisitive and seeing like where are the ads and everything you said, follow through, go through the funnel, see where they're posting. You can get anecdotal evidence on what people are actually paying for that ad. However, what someone else is paying is not necessarily what you will pay. There are some ad platforms where it's all about how much per click or per impression, they're just in it for the maximum dollar. Other ones, without mentioning my name, introduce the concept of quality of ad and thus they might take a lower price for an ad or an ad space from a company that they know is established and their readers, their customers are happy with. Then they would take from you as a newbie. One of the joys is the world you're marketing into now is you no longer have to make a massive commitment to buy TV time. Thus, you're already out six figures before you know anything. You really can run small test ads as you're learning and test. If you think you're learning something about the marketplace, you are able to make small investments to validate or test those theories. Good. Do I cover that? Yes, good stuff. Okay. Now, yeah, this is the first step of the simple writing system. This will be the first chance you have to do an exercise. The exercise details are in your workbook. They're on the page somewhere here depending on how you're watching this video. I'll strongly recommend do the exercise. I know a lot of people, like they think they want to get through all the material as soon as possible. You could do it that way. You're better off. The simple writing system in many ways does build. What's going to come in following sections is based on what came before. So I would strongly recommend you take the time and as much time as you need to look at the exercise and invest some time here, do a little bit of market research or at least do a little bit and then plan what you'll do next before you dive in. If you're in the coaching program, again, don't forget, post your exercise and get feedback from your coach on this. Very, very important. Okay. The questions that I want you to answer, and again, this is very simple, especially if you're in the coaching program, you can get feedback from your coach. But if you're not, just look into this. Is there a large enough market? Answer that question for yourself. Answer who are the major competitors? Just go online and find out or find out who the offline competitors are. And finally, what are some of the marketing methods they use? How is your competition reaching the prospects in the market that you're after? It's a nice way to start. It doesn't have to take you a long time. You should be able to get through this fairly quickly, but it's a great way to get started in this detective mode, which you're going to learn to love.
